explanation: The statement "The autopilot shall prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" implies that whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, prop_0 must also be true. This is a global requirement, so it is expressed using the 'G' (Globally) operator. The condition 'prop_1 and prop_2' translates to 'prop_1 & prop_2', and 'shall prop_0' means 'prop_0'. The 'when' indicates an implication.

The statement 'The autopilot shall prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2' translates to 'Globally, if prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true'. This is a direct implication enforced at all times.

The statement "The autopilot shall prop_0 when the prop_1 and prop_2" implies a continuous requirement. 'Shall' indicates that the condition must always hold, which translates to a global (G) property. 'When' indicates an implication: if the condition (prop_1 and prop_2) is true, then the action (prop_0) must also be true at that same time. Therefore, the LTL formula states that globally, if prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true.

The statement 'The autopilot shall prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2' implies that whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, prop_0 must also be true. This relationship must hold globally, meaning at all times. Therefore, it translates to a global implication: G ((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0).

The statement "The autopilot shall prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" indicates a global implication. It means that at all times (G), if the condition (prop_1 and prop_2) is met, then prop_0 must be true. Thus, it translates to G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0).
The statement "The autopilot shall prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" implies that if both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true. This is a direct implication.

The phrase 'prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2' implies a global implication. It means that whenever the condition (prop_1 AND prop_2) holds, prop_0 must also hold at that same time. In LTL, 'whenever A, then B' is commonly translated as G(A -> B). Thus, G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0) captures this relationship.

The statement "The autopilot shall prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" implies a standing requirement that whenever the conditions prop_1 and prop_2 are met, prop_0 must also be true at that very same time. This is best represented by a global implication, G(condition -> action).

The statement "prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" implies that whenever the conjunction of 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' is true, 'prop_0' must also be true. This is a global condition, meaning it must hold at all times. Therefore, it translates to a global implication: 'Globally, if (prop_1 and prop_2) then prop_0'.

The statement "A shall B when C" translates to a global implication: G (C -> B). In this case, A is implicit (autopilot), B is 'prop_0', and C is 'prop_1 and prop_2'. Therefore, the LTL formula is 'Globally, if prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true'.
